metadata.title: Why Quantum Breaks RSA
metadata.description: Interactive dashboard comparing classical RSA vulnerability to Shor's algorithm vs post-quantum cryptography resilience
metadata.ogDescription: Interactive dashboard showing how quantum computers break RSA encryption and why post-quantum cryptography is the solution

header.subtitle: Interactive comparison: Classical cryptography vs Post-Quantum security

footer.disclaimer: Educational simulation — qubit estimates are approximate based on current research

qubitSlider.heading: Qubit Count
qubitSlider.description: Drag to simulate quantum computer scaling
qubitSlider.mlkemExplanation: No qubit threshold exists — lattice problems have no known efficient quantum solution

shorPanel.heading: Classical RSA / ECC
shorPanel.descPart2: to factor large numbers exponentially faster than any classical algorithm. The key step is
shorPanel.descPart4: . This breaks RSA, whose security relies on the difficulty of factoring.
shorPanel.inputLabel: ENTER A NUMBER TO FACTOR (Shor's Simulation)
shorPanel.idlePrompt: Enter a number above to simulate Shor's algorithm
shorPanel.encryptionBroken: RSA key factored by quantum computer — encryption broken
shorPanel.couldNotFactor: Could not factor {n} in this run
shorPanel.probabilistic: Shor's algorithm is probabilistic — the chosen random values didn't yield factors this time. Try again!

pqcPanel.heading: Post-Quantum (ML-KEM)
pqcPanel.descPart3: lattice problem. Even quantum computers with Grover's algorithm only achieve a √n speedup — not enough to break it.
pqcPanel.groverDesc: Unlike Shor's exponential speedup for factoring, Grover's search only provides a quadratic advantage. 2^128 → 2^64 operations.
pqcPanel.noKnownDesc: The Shortest Vector Problem in high-dimensional lattices has no efficient quantum algorithm — unlike integer factoring.
pqcPanel.noiseDesc: adds Gaussian noise to lattice computations. Even with quantum superposition, the noise prevents extracting the secret key.
pqcPanel.classicalBarAriaLabel: Classical brute force: full bar representing 2 to the power of 128 operations
pqcPanel.groverBarAriaLabel: Grover quantum search: half bar representing 2 to the power of 64 operations
pqcPanel.infeasible: Still 1.8×10^19 operations — completely infeasible even for quantum computers
pqcPanel.noQuantumAlgo: No quantum algorithm can efficiently solve the lattice problem

quantumCircuit.desc: Hadamard, Oracle, Inverse QFT, and Measure

lattice.label: LATTICE PROBLEM (SVP)
lattice.title: Lattice visualization showing the Shortest Vector Problem
lattice.desc: A grid of {size}×{size} points with the closest vector highlighted. {attempts} quantum search attempts shown, all missing the target — demonstrating that Grover's algorithm only provides square root speedup.

quiz.excellentMessage: Excellent! You have strong knowledge of quantum cryptography.
quiz.goodMessage: Good effort! Review the topics you missed to strengthen your understanding.
quiz.keepLearningMessage: Keep learning! The visualizations above can help build your understanding.

questions.question: What mathematical problem does RSA encryption rely on for its security?
questions.explanation: RSA security depends on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers. Classical computers cannot efficiently factor large semiprimes, which is what makes RSA secure today.

questions.question: What does Shor's algorithm efficiently solve on a quantum computer?
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm can factor integers and compute discrete logarithms in polynomial time on a quantum computer, breaking RSA and many other public-key cryptosystems.

questions.question: Approximately how many logical qubits are estimated to break RSA-2048?
questions.explanation: Current research estimates that roughly 4,000 error-corrected logical qubits would be needed to factor a 2048-bit RSA key using Shor's algorithm, though physical qubit requirements are much higher due to error correction overhead.

questions.question: What is ML-KEM (formerly CRYSTALS-Kyber)?
options: A NIST-standardized post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism
questions.explanation: ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism), standardized as FIPS 203 in August 2024, is a post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism based on the hardness of lattice problems.

questions.question: Why are lattice-based cryptographic schemes considered quantum-resistant?
options: They use quantum entanglement for key exchange
No known quantum algorithm can efficiently solve lattice problems like SVP
They require more qubits than will ever be built
They are based on one-time pads which are information-theoretically secure
questions.explanation: Lattice-based schemes rely on problems like the Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) and Learning With Errors (LWE). No known quantum algorithm, including Shor's, can efficiently solve these problems.

questions.question: What is a "harvest now, decrypt later" attack?
options: Stealing encryption keys from quantum computers
Capturing encrypted data today to decrypt it once quantum computers are available
Using quantum computers to break real-time communications
A brute-force attack that runs overnight
questions.explanation: Adversaries can record encrypted traffic today and store it until sufficiently powerful quantum computers exist to decrypt it. This "harvest now, decrypt later" threat is why migrating to post-quantum cryptography is urgent even before large-scale quantum computers are built.

questions.question: Which quantum phenomenon does Shor's algorithm exploit to find the period of a function?
options: Quantum superposition and interference via the Quantum Fourier Transform
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm uses superposition to evaluate a function at many inputs simultaneously, then applies the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) to extract the period through constructive and destructive interference.

questions.question: What NIST process led to the standardization of post-quantum cryptographic algorithms?
options: The Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization Project started in 2016
questions.explanation: NIST began its Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization Project in 2016, evaluating submissions over multiple rounds. The first standards (FIPS 203, 204, 205) were published in August 2024.
